title: Retention Check: Windows Telemetry — Seeing the Full Picture

questions.q: What does Event 4624's LogonType field indicate, and what are the values for interactive, network, and RDP logons?
questions.a: LogonType indicates the authentication method used. 2 = interactive (local console). 3 = network (SMB/WMI — lateral movement path). 10 = RemoteInteractive (RDP). Type 3 from an unexpected source IP is the primary lateral movement detection signature.

questions.q: What does Sysmon Event 10 detect and what GrantedAccess value indicates LSASS credential dumping?
questions.a: Event 10 (ProcessAccess) fires when one process opens another's memory space. GrantedAccess=0x1010 or 0x1410 includes PROCESS_VM_READ, sufficient for credential dumping. TargetImage=lsass.exe with these access rights is the detection signature.

questions.q: What is the DCSync detection signature in AD events, and what distinguishes it from legitimate replication?
questions.a: Event 4662, Object=domainDNS, AccessMask=0x100 (DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All). Legitimate replication is performed by domain controller computer accounts (ending in $). DCSync is flagged when the requesting account is NOT a DC computer account.

questions.q: Why is ProcessGUID preferred over PID for correlating Sysmon events?
questions.a: Windows PIDs are 16-bit values reused after process exit. In a high-activity environment, a PID may be reused within the correlation window, causing false attribution. ProcessGUID is unique for each process for the system's lifetime.

questions.q: Name the four-source Windows kill chain for detecting PsExec lateral movement.
questions.a: Sysmon Event 1 (process creation of psexec on source) → Sysmon Event 3 (network connection to destination, linked by ProcessGUID) → Windows Security Event 4624 (logon Type 3 on destination, linked by SessionGUID) → AD Event 4769 (Kerberos ticket issued if domain context used).
